Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Early Modern

Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz

1646 – 1716

Monads, petites perceptions, and a psychology built entirely from within.

Leibniz’s philosophy of mind is best reconstructed as a graded, theistic, anti-reductive theory of active perspectives. Reality is ultimately composed of simple, immaterial, internally active unities—monads—whose basic life consists in perception and appetition. Consciousness is not the essence of mind; it is a higher, more distinct, or reflexive form of perception. Souls are monads with sensation and memory. Minds, or rational spirits, are souls capable of necessary truths, self-consciousness, moral personality, and fellowship with God. God’s mind is not an optional theological appendix to the system; it is the archetype and metaphysical ground of cognition, order, possibility, harmony, and normativity. Angels, when placed within the system, belong to the class of finite rational spirits and therefore illuminate the upper range of created mentality rather than standing outside Leibniz’s philosophy of mind.

1. The basic vocabulary: monad, perception, appetition, soul, mind

Leibniz’s mature account begins with the monad. A monad is a simple substance: it has no parts, is not extended, and cannot be explained as a mechanical aggregate. Monads have “no windows,” meaning that no real causal item enters or exits them; their changes arise from internal principles rather than from literal causal influx from other substances. This does not make them empty points. Each monad has qualitative states, and those states are perceptions: internal representations of multiplicity in unity.

In Principles of Nature and Grace, Leibniz gives a compact definition useful for philosophy of mind: simple substances may be called lives, souls, or minds depending on their grade, and their internal qualities are perceptions, while their actions are appetitions, the tendencies by which they pass from one perception to another. This establishes the two primitive psychological functions of all monads: representing and striving.

The crucial terminological point is that Leibniz does not use “soul,” “mind,” and “monad” as exact synonyms in every context. In a very broad sense, every monad is soul-like because every monad perceives and appetitively changes. In a stricter sense, “soul” applies to monads with more distinct perception and memory, especially animal souls. In the strictest sense, “mind” or “spirit” applies to rational souls: beings capable of necessary truths, reflection, self-consciousness, moral responsibility, and relation to God. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy summarizes the same hierarchy: all fundamental beings are mind-like, but only God, human souls, and angels count as “minds” in the stricter self-conscious and rational sense.

This matters because Leibniz is not simply saying “everything is conscious.” He is closer to a panpsychist or, more precisely, panexperientialist metaphysics of primitive perception, combined with a graded account of consciousness. Francophone scholarship makes this point sharply: Jean-Pascal Anfray describes Leibniz’s philosophy of mind as at once traditional, radical, and novel—traditional because it defends immaterial soul, immortality, and innate principles; radical because matter is grounded in immaterial units; and novel because monads are defined by perception and appetition, yielding a panpsychist implication.

2. The unity argument: why mind cannot be reduced to mechanism

Leibniz’s central anti-materialist argument is not merely that matter seems inert or that mechanism has not yet explained consciousness. His deeper claim is that perception requires true unity, whereas matter, taken as extended mechanism, is an aggregate. A machine can have parts acting on parts, but an aggregate of parts does not itself explain a single subject of experience. In the famous “mill” passage of the Monadology, he asks us to imagine entering an enlarged machine whose parts operate mechanically. We would find pushes, pulls, wheels, and motions, but never perception itself. Therefore perception must be sought in a simple substance, not in a composite machine.

The Stanford Encyclopedia article on Leibniz’s philosophy of mind highlights the structure of this argument: perception and consciousness require a genuine unity or single “I,” while matter cannot provide that unity. This makes Leibniz an opponent of reductive materialism, but not a simple Cartesian dualist. He rejects the idea that extended material substance is metaphysically ultimate, while also rejecting literal interaction between mind and body.

This is the first major principle for extracting a Leibnizian philosophy of mind: the subject of experience is not an emergent aggregate but a metaphysically simple center of representation. An organism’s body may be complex, and its nervous system may display lawful mechanisms, but the unity of conscious or proto-conscious perspective belongs to a dominant simple substance. This is Leibniz’s way of avoiding a problem that later theories of consciousness still face: how many physical or functional parts become one subject.

3. Perception as expression: every mind is a perspective on the universe

For Leibniz, perception is not a little picture in the head. It is an expression of the world by a simple substance. Every monad represents the entire universe from its own point of view. The difference among monads is not that some are related to the world and others are not; rather, each monad expresses the whole, but with different degrees of distinctness. Some things are represented clearly; most are represented confusedly.

This perspectival theory is one of Leibniz’s most powerful contributions to philosophy of mind. It means that mentality is inherently world-involving, yet not dependent on external causal input. A mind’s perceptions arise from within, but what they express is the ordered universe. The “inside” of the monad is not cut off from the world; it is the world expressed from a determinate perspective.

The body plays a decisive role in this perspectival structure. A created soul represents the entire universe, but it represents its own body more distinctly than it represents distant things. Through that body, it represents the rest of the universe. This is why embodiment is not incidental for Leibniz. A body is the perspectival focus through which a created monad expresses the world with greater clarity in one region.

This also clarifies Leibniz’s representationalism. In the New Essays, he says that ideas are immediate inner objects of the soul that express the nature or qualities of things. External things are not directly injected into the mind; they are represented according to the soul’s internal law. God alone, Leibniz says there, can be an immediate external object of the soul.

The resulting view is neither naïve realism nor subjective idealism in a simple modern sense. Leibniz is a perspectival realist about the order expressed and an internalist about the production of mental states. Minds do not receive perceptions by physical impact; they unfold perceptions according to their own nature, in harmony with the world.

4. Consciousness is not the essence of mind

Leibniz’s most modern-looking doctrine is his insistence that perception and consciousness must be distinguished. In Monadology §14, he explicitly criticizes the Cartesians for confusing perception with apperception or consciousness, thereby neglecting perceptions of which we are not aware. This is one of the points at which Leibniz decisively breaks with a simple Cartesian conception of mind as transparent to itself.

The Stanford Encyclopedia’s article on seventeenth-century theories of consciousness notes that Leibniz may be the first philosopher to develop an explicit theory of consciousness in this sense, including the term “apperception.” In Principles of Nature and Grace, perception is the internal representative state of the monad, while apperception is consciousness or reflective knowledge of that internal state; not every soul has it, and even the same soul does not always have it.

Leibniz’s famous doctrine of petites perceptions, or minute perceptions, is the core of this theory. In the New Essays, he uses the example of the roar of the sea: we consciously hear the roar, but that conscious sound is composed of innumerable tiny perceptions of individual waves, each too small to be noticed on its own. A hundred thousand “nothings,” he argues, could not make a real conscious sound; therefore the small perceptions must be real, even if unnoticed.

This produces a graded model of mind. Conscious awareness is not created from nothing when a threshold is crossed. Rather, unconscious perceptions become conscious when they acquire sufficient distinctness, intensity, contrast, integration, or attentional prominence. In the New Essays, Leibniz also argues that tiny perceptions determine our behavior, generate vague moods and inclinations, and form the background of desire and disquiet.

There is an important interpretive dispute here. Some readings treat Leibnizian apperception as a higher-order reflective act: to be conscious of a perception is to have a perception of that perception. Other readings treat apperception as a first-order increase in distinctness or attention. The Stanford Encyclopedia explicitly notes this debate.

Non-English scholarship is especially useful on this point. Antoine Charbonneau, writing in French on perception, apperception, and consciousness, argues that Leibniz can grant apperception to non-rational animals while reserving reflection for rational spirits. Roberto Casales García, writing in Spanish, similarly distinguishes sensitive apperception from conscientia and argues against simply confining apperception to rational spirits. These interpretations help resolve an apparent tension in Leibniz: animals can have conscious or quasi-conscious sensory awareness without possessing rational self-reflection.

A rigorous reconstruction should therefore distinguish at least four levels. First, perception: any internal representational state of a monad. Second, sensation: a more distinct perception, often involving organic embodiment and memory. Third, apperception: awareness, noticing, or conscious uptake of a perception. Fourth, reflection or conscientia: rational self-consciousness, the capacity to think “I,” to know oneself, and to grasp necessary truths. This layered reading fits the primary texts and avoids flattening Leibniz into either Cartesian transparency or contemporary unconscious cognition alone.

5. Appetition: mind as active striving, not passive reception

The second primitive feature of monadic life is appetition. Perceptions do not merely sit inside a monad; each state tends toward another. Appetition is the inner principle of transition from one perception to the next. It is the root of desire, attention, impulse, inclination, and will.

This makes Leibniz’s mind intrinsically active. The mind is not a blank slate passively written upon by external objects. In the New Essays, Leibniz explicitly rejects the fiction of a pure blank page and treats innate ideas and principles as dispositions, tendencies, or virtualities within the mind.

Leibniz’s psychology of action is continuous with his doctrine of minute perceptions. Our choices are shaped by innumerable small inclinations, many too subtle to be noticed. Even when we seem calmly indifferent, Leibniz denies that the will is ever in a perfectly balanced state. Tiny perceptions and appetitions incline us one way or another.

This does not make human action externally necessitated in a crude deterministic sense. Leibniz’s account of freedom is based on spontaneity and intelligence. The agent acts from its own internal principle, not by being pushed from outside. A rational agent is free when its action expresses its own reasons, perceptions, and appetitions in an intelligent way. In the New System, Leibniz even describes substances as “spiritual” or “formal” automata, adding that in rational cases they are free.

This is a distinctive alternative to both libertarian indeterminism and mechanistic determinism. Human agency is neither random nor externally forced. It is internally generated, reason-responsive, and continuous with the appetitive structure of all monads.

6. Animal souls, human minds, and the hierarchy of mental life

Leibniz’s philosophy of mind is hierarchical. At the lowest level are bare monads or entelechies: simple substances with perception and appetition, but perceptions so confused that they lack sensation, memory, or awareness in any ordinary sense. Above them are souls, especially animal souls, which have sensation and memory. Above animal souls are rational souls or minds, which possess reason, self-consciousness, knowledge of necessary truths, and moral personality.

Animals are not machines in the Cartesian sense. They have perception, memory, and empirical expectation. Leibniz says that memory imitates reason in animals: a dog that has been beaten with a stick may expect pain when it sees the stick again. But animals do not grasp necessary truths or universal reasons. Humans, by contrast, can reflect, think “I,” know themselves, and know God.

The difference between human and animal minds is therefore not that humans have mental life and animals do not. It is that humans possess a rational and reflective grade of mental life. Leibniz’s position is thus anti-Cartesian about animals but not egalitarian about all mentality. The animal has soul; the human has mind in the strict sense.

The body of a living being is itself an organized aggregate containing subordinate monads. The animal or human soul is the dominant monad of an organic body, but the body is not a dead lump. Leibniz famously insists that nature is full of life, and that there is nothing absolutely dead or barren in the universe.

This doctrine also changes birth and death. Leibniz denies absolute generation and absolute destruction of living beings. Animals and souls do not simply begin from nothing or vanish into nothing; they undergo transformations, enfoldings, and unfoldings. Death is not annihilation of the soul, though it may involve a loss of the body’s previous organization and a descent into more confused perception.

7. Mind and body: pre-established harmony rather than interaction

Leibniz’s account of the mind-body relation is one of the most distinctive parts of his system. Because monads have no windows, the soul does not literally act on the body, and the body does not literally act on the soul. Yet mental and bodily states correspond with extraordinary precision. Leibniz explains this by pre-established harmony: God creates each substance with an internal law such that its states unfold in coordination with all others.

This is not occasionalism. Leibniz rejects the view that God must intervene at each moment to make mind and body correspond. Instead, God’s original creative act gives substances internal natures that harmonize without continual miraculous adjustment. The body proceeds according to efficient causes and mechanical laws; the soul proceeds according to final causes, appetitions, ends, and means. Each series is autonomous in its own order, yet the two are coordinated.

In Monadology §§78–81, Leibniz states the doctrine in its mature form: souls act according to final causes, bodies according to efficient causes, and the two realms harmonize so that bodies act as if there were no souls and souls as if there were no bodies.

The philosophy-of-mind payoff is considerable. Leibniz avoids reductive materialism because mental unity cannot be mechanically produced. He avoids Cartesian interactionism because there is no intelligible transfer of motion or causal force from an immaterial soul into an extended body. He avoids occasionalist ad hoc intervention because the harmony is built into the world’s original order. The cost is that mental causation is internal rather than cross-substantial: my desire does not physically push my arm, but my desire belongs to the soul’s final-causal series, while my arm movement belongs to the body’s efficient-causal series, and both series correspond.

This should not be confused with epiphenomenalism. The soul’s appetitions are real causes within the soul’s own order. What Leibniz denies is not mental efficacy as such, but inter-substantial causal influx. Mental life is not an inert shadow of body; it is an autonomous teleological order harmonized with bodily mechanism.

8. God’s mind: not a theological distraction but the architecture of the system

The mind of God is indispensable to Leibniz’s philosophy of mind. God is not merely one very powerful mind among others. In the Monadology, God is the primary unity, the original simple substance, the necessary being whose understanding is the region of eternal truths and whose will chooses the best among possible worlds.

Leibniz’s created monads have three basic aspects: their metaphysical basis or unity, their perceptive power, and their appetitive power. In God, these correspond to Power, Knowledge, and Will, but in an infinite and perfect form. This means that divine mentality is the archetype of monadic mentality, not a later religious decoration. Created minds are finite analogues of divine intellect and will.

God also grounds the coordination of minds. Since monads do not literally interact, the agreement among their perceptions must be explained by a common source. Pre-established harmony is intelligible only because God creates the world as an ordered system of mutually corresponding perspectives. Removing God from Leibniz’s system does not merely remove theology; it removes the metaphysical ground of universal mental-world correspondence.

God’s cognition also supplies the limiting ideal of knowledge. Each created monad represents the whole universe, but only from a finite perspective and with limited distinctness. Leibniz explicitly says that if each monad represented the whole universe distinctly, each would be a deity. God alone has the fully distinct, non-perspectivally complete grasp of the whole.

This gives Leibniz a vertical theory of mind. At the bottom are confused perceptions; above them are sensation and memory; above them are rational self-consciousness and necessary truths; at the summit is divine understanding, in which eternal truths, possibilities, and the chosen world are perfectly known. A comprehensive Leibnizian philosophy of mind must therefore include theology as cognitive metaphysics.

9. Angels and finite created spirits

Angels should be handled carefully because they are not peripheral to the category of rational spirit. Standard secondary treatments of Leibniz’s philosophy of mind include angels alongside God and human souls as examples of minds in the strict sense: self-conscious, rational beings rather than merely perceptive monads.

Within Leibniz’s mature monadology, however, angels should not be treated as Cartesian disembodied minds in the same sense as God. Leibniz says that only God is completely without body. Created monads, including rational spirits, are associated with bodies, even if the body is subtle, transformed, or unlike ordinary human embodiment.

This is philosophically important. Angels, if included, mark the upper boundary of created mind: finite rational perspectives with greater clarity than ordinary human beings, but still finite, perspectival, and dependent on God. They are not mini-Gods. They remain within the order of created monads, and therefore within the metaphysics of perception, appetition, embodiment, and harmony.

Angels also help explain Leibniz’s doctrine of the City of God. In the final sections of the Monadology, rational minds are not merely natural substances; they are citizens in a moral community governed by God. God is not only architect of the physical order but monarch and lawgiver of the moral order. The harmony between the physical realm of nature and the moral realm of grace is central to Leibniz’s final account of rational mind.

This is where Leibniz’s philosophy of mind becomes a philosophy of rational community. A mind is not only a center of representation but also a potential participant in divine society: capable of knowing the system of the universe, imitating divine order architectonically, and entering moral relation with God and other spirits.

10. Innateness, self-consciousness, and personal identity

Leibniz’s theory of mind is also a theory of innate structure. He rejects Locke’s blank slate. The mind does not receive its basic intellectual contents from outside; it unfolds them from within as dispositions, tendencies, or virtual structures. Experience occasions the development of knowledge but does not implant it by external causal transfer.

Reason distinguishes human minds from animal souls. In Monadology §§29–30, Leibniz says that knowledge of necessary and eternal truths distinguishes us from mere animals and raises us to knowledge of ourselves and of God. Reflection makes us think of what is called “I.”

Personal identity depends on more than explicit memory. Leibniz’s doctrine of tiny perceptions allows him to say that the soul preserves traces of its whole past, even where conscious recollection fails. The continuity of the individual is grounded in the continuity of the soul’s perceptual series, while moral or personal identity is made manifest through apperception, memory, and reflective self-awareness.

This gives Leibniz a layered theory of the self. At the deepest level, the self is the enduring individual substance whose complete concept contains its history. At the psychological level, the self is the continuity of perceptions and appetitions. At the personal and moral level, the self is the rational being capable of saying “I,” remembering, deliberating, and being accountable before God and other rational spirits.

11. Multilingual reception: useful interpretive tensions

The Anglophone reception often emphasizes Leibniz’s anti-materialism, pre-established harmony, representationalism, and relation to contemporary panpsychism. That is justified, but non-English discussions bring out different fault lines.

Francophone work is especially attentive to the tension between Leibniz’s traditional doctrine of the soul and the radical implications of monadology. Anfray’s French overview stresses that Leibniz simultaneously preserves traditional theses about immaterial soul and transforms them by making perception and appetition universal features of monads.

French and Spanish discussions are also valuable on apperception. Charbonneau argues that Leibniz can attribute apperception to animals while reserving reflection for spirits. Casales García similarly distinguishes sensitive apperception from conscientia, presenting Leibniz as intermediate between higher-order theories of consciousness and first-order accounts based on perceptual distinctness. This is not a minor technical debate: it affects whether Leibniz is read as making consciousness essentially rational, or as allowing non-rational conscious awareness in animals.

German reception brings another emphasis: Leibniz as a source for later psychology. Jochen Fahrenberg’s German study of Leibniz’s influence on Wilhelm Wundt identifies themes such as the law of continuity, harmony, individuality, Eigenaktivität or self-activity, perception and apperception, psychophysical parallelism, consciousness, will, and monism. This reception often secularizes Leibniz, but the secularized version should not obscure the fact that, for Leibniz himself, God, grace, and the moral community of spirits are architectonic parts of the theory.

The most productive synthesis is therefore not to choose between theological Leibniz and proto-cognitive-science Leibniz. Leibniz’s theory of mind is both: a systematic theory of unconscious perception, consciousness, representation, and psychophysical correlation, embedded in a metaphysics of divine intellect, created spirits, and moral order.

12. A reconstructed Leibnizian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive Leibnizian philosophy of mind can be stated as the following theses.

First, the subject thesis: every genuine subject of experience must be a true unity, not an aggregate. Mechanism may explain bodily processes, but it cannot by itself explain the unity of perception.

Second, the primitive mentality thesis: the basic features of monadic life are perception and appetition. Mentality, in the broadest sense, is not identical with consciousness; it is representational and appetitive activity.

Third, the gradation thesis: mental life comes in degrees. Bare monads have confused perception; animal souls have sensation and memory; rational minds have reflection, necessary truths, and self-consciousness; God has perfect understanding and will.

Fourth, the unconscious perception thesis: unconscious perceptions are real and indispensable. They explain continuity in nature, the emergence of conscious awareness by degrees, background moods, inclinations, and the persistence of personal identity beneath explicit memory.

Fifth, the perspectival expression thesis: every monad expresses the entire universe from its point of view. Created minds are finite perspectives on the whole, not isolated containers of private mental items.

Sixth, the embodiment thesis: every created mind is associated with a body, and the body is the region of the universe that the soul represents most distinctly. God alone is completely without body.

Seventh, the anti-interactionist thesis: mind and body do not exchange causal influence. Their correspondence is explained by pre-established harmony, not by material reduction, Cartesian interaction, or occasional divine intervention at every moment.

Eighth, the teleological agency thesis: mental causality is final-causal and appetitive. Minds act according to ends, reasons, inclinations, and perceived goods, while bodies operate according to efficient mechanical causes.

Ninth, the rational spirit thesis: a mind in the strict sense is not merely conscious but rational, self-conscious, capable of necessary truths, and morally accountable. Human beings and angels fall under this category as finite created spirits; God is the infinite and original spirit.

Tenth, the divine ground thesis: God’s mind grounds eternal truths, possible worlds, the choice of the best world, and the harmony among all created perspectives. Divine cognition is therefore internal to the system’s philosophy of mind.

Eleventh, the moral-community thesis: rational minds are not only cognitive subjects but citizens of the City of God. The final meaning of mind is not exhausted by representation or consciousness; it includes moral relation, happiness, justice, and participation in a divine order.

Twelfth, the continuity thesis: there are no absolute leaps from non-mind to mind, from unconsciousness to consciousness, or from life to death. Nature proceeds by degrees, transformations, and enfoldings. This is why Leibniz can combine panpsychist metaphysics, animal psychology, human rationality, and divine intellect in one continuous system.

13. Philosophical assessment

Leibniz’s system has several enduring strengths. It gives a powerful argument that consciousness requires unity. It provides a sophisticated theory of unconscious perception long before modern depth psychology or cognitive science. It avoids reducing mind to mechanism while still respecting lawful bodily processes. It treats animals as genuinely ensouled rather than as machines. It integrates cognition, desire, agency, embodiment, and normativity into one metaphysical framework.

Its difficulties are equally serious. Pre-established harmony depends heavily on God’s original ordering of the world. Without God, the correspondence among monads risks becoming unexplained. The doctrine of dominant monads avoids making consciousness an aggregate, but it raises questions about how the dominant monad relates to the subordinate monads of the body. The distinction between perception, apperception, consciousness, and reflection is textually unstable enough to sustain continuing disagreement.

The most faithful extraction is therefore not “Leibniz was a dualist,” “Leibniz was a panpsychist,” or “Leibniz anticipated cognitive science,” though each phrase captures something. The fuller view is this: Leibniz offers a theistic, pluralistic idealism of active, embodied, perspectival centers of representation, ordered by divine wisdom, graded from unconscious perception through animal awareness and human or angelic rationality up to the perfect mind of God. That is his comprehensive philosophy of mind.
